Title: The Glass Lighthouse
Author: Marisol Aguilar
My Review: A novel that taught me grief is just love with no place left to go. I read it slowly over a month, and most chapters required a long walk afterward to recover. The prose has the patience of someone who knows what they are doing — paragraphs that begin in a kitchen end up describing the entire history of a marriage. The lighthouse is barely a metaphor, which is the trick: the book trusts the object to do its work without translation. I finished it twice in the same year.

Title: Why The Tides Forget
Author: Hira Banerjee
My Review: Elegant and quiet. A small book that mostly succeeds. The middle section sags but the ending earns its restraint.

Title: Cathedral of Salt
Author: Elena Vidal
My Review: The only book this year that made me miss my flight on purpose. A small town in northern Spain wakes up to find the cathedral has become entirely transparent, and from then on every confession is also a public broadcast. Vidal uses this device with terrifying patience — chapters pass before anyone in the town acknowledges what has happened. The novel is really about the cost of being seen, and what we owe to the people who refuse to look away. I underlined twenty separate paragraphs and have already lent it to four people.

Title: The Quiet Plural
Author: Tomas Hovden
My Review: Promising premise, mid execution. The middle hundred pages could be cut.

Title: Foundations of Synthetic Worlds
Author: Ravi Subramanian
My Review: The book I want to give to every junior engineer who tells me they are bored. Subramanian and Park argue that simulation is now a literacy, not a discipline — that the gap between modeling a city, modeling a market, and modeling a mind is mostly cosmetic. The chapter on emergent governance in long-running game worlds is worth the price of the book by itself. Best read alongside Vinge and a long bike ride.

Title: Birds of the Inland Sea
Author: Margit Sundgren
My Review: A naturalist's slow inventory of the Baltic, written before the seas warmed. Worth reading just for the chapter on cormorants, which is one of the best pieces of nature writing I have ever encountered. Sundgren writes like someone who knows she has time.

Title: The Returners
Author: Jules Pemberton
My Review: Did not work for me. The prose is fine, the politics are sharper than the characters, and the ending arrives too neatly.

Title: A Surveyor's Notebook
Author: Anaïs Maréchal
My Review: Maréchal walked the entire 1100km of the old French triangulation chain on foot, in stages, over six years. The book is part survey log, part lyric memoir, part argument that we have lost the patience to belong to a landscape. The chapter where she sleeps inside the ruined trig point above Roussillon is one I have read out loud to friends. A small, beautifully built book that does not want to be a series.

Title: The Fifth Argument
Author: Yusuf Idris
My Review: A clear and patient introduction to the late Frankfurt school for people allergic to the early Frankfurt school. I learned more from chapters six and seven than from the entire previous decade of online discourse on the same subjects.

Title: Cold Mailroom
Author: Anita Korhonen
My Review: A perfectly fine procedural with one fantastic supporting character.

Title: On Long Sentences
Author: Idris Bah
My Review: The best book on prose rhythm I have ever read. Bah argues that the long sentence is a moral instrument — it asks the reader to stay, to keep their breath, to refuse the cheap relief of the period. He demonstrates this, of course, by writing the entire book in sentences that are themselves moral instruments. I will reread this every couple of years for the rest of my life.

Title: Quiet Compute
Author: Helena Park
My Review: A focused argument that the future of computing is small, local, and embarrassed. The middle chapter on edge inference is the clearest writing on the subject I have seen.

Title: Bridges of the Estuary
Author: Owen Carrick
My Review: A model of what a single-subject history book can do. Carrick treats the bridges of one estuary as a 200-year argument about who pays, who plans, and who gets to cross. The chapter on the 1902 collapse is essential reading for anyone who works on infrastructure of any kind.

Title: Stillwater
Author: Sora Tanaka
My Review: A perfect novel about boredom. Tanaka takes a year in the life of a small-town piano tuner and refuses to give it any of the usual escape valves — no affair, no death, no reckoning, no return to the city. By the final chapter the reader has accidentally learned to love the same things the tuner loves. I do not know how she does this.

Title: Field Notes from a Synthetic City
Author: Mei Lin Park
My Review: Park spent four years inside a private simulation of a real mid-sized city, working alongside the planners who actually run the place. The book is the first honest account I have read of what it is like to be the test subject inside your own forecasting tool. The chapter on the day the simulation predicted the bus strike before the union had voted is one I will remember for a long time.

Title: Brief Histories of the Number Seven
Author: Idris Bah
My Review: Slighter than On Long Sentences but still essential. Reads in a single weekend.

Title: The Hollow Crown of Vys
Author: Ari Solberg
My Review: Competent epic fantasy with a magic system that never quite earns its complications.

Title: Children of the Mainframe
Author: Petra Klein
My Review: A generational history of the women who actually ran Europe's mainframe shops between 1968 and 1992. Klein interviewed 87 of them. The book is part oral history, part technical archaeology, part argument that we have systematically forgotten the operators while remembering only the architects. Heartbreaking and necessary.

Title: The Ninth Warehouse
Author: Liesl Brandt
My Review: A small mystery built around an obsolete logistics protocol. Funnier and stranger than its premise suggests.

Title: On the Ethics of Slow Software
Author: Hadiya Osei
My Review: The most important book I have read this year. Osei argues that the ethics of software are inseparable from the ethics of attention — that any product which optimizes for engagement is making a moral claim about how its users should spend the rest of their lives. The chapter on the duty of friction is one I have already pressed on three different people. Read this if you build anything.

Title: Marrow
Author: Devika Rao
My Review: A short, dense novel about a transplant surgeon who begins to dream the lives of her donors. Less mystical than the premise suggests; more a quiet study of how the body remembers what the mind cannot.

Title: The Last Map of Hokkaido
Author: Sora Tanaka
My Review: Tanaka's second novel is even quieter than the first, which I would not have thought possible. A retired cartographer is asked to update the official map of the prefecture and discovers that several villages no longer wish to be on it. The book never raises its voice. It is also one of the best novels I have ever read about consent.

Title: Hot Iron Cold Fire
Author: Ari Solberg
My Review: A worse Vys with a worse magic system.

Title: Working in the Long Now
Author: Marin Espinoza
My Review: The book that finally made me stop apologizing for spending years on a single project. Espinoza is the rare writer who can argue for slowness without ever sounding precious about it. The chapter on what she calls the seven-year contract is now part of how I plan my work.

Title: The Telegraph House
Author: Marisol Aguilar
My Review: Aguilar's quieter novel. Worthwhile but not Lighthouse.

Title: Distributed Cities
Author: Ravi Subramanian
My Review: A practical companion to Field Notes. Less lyrical, more operational; useful if you actually have to build the systems Park describes.

Title: Estuary
Author: Owen Carrick
My Review: Carrick's masterwork. He spent ten years on this book and you can feel every one of them on the page. A history of a single tidal estuary across nine centuries, told through the people who have made their living from it. The final chapter, written in the present tense from a small boat at the mouth of the river, is one of the great pieces of contemporary nature writing.

Title: The Quiet Plural II
Author: Tomas Hovden
My Review: A sequel that explains why nobody asked for one.

Title: The Cartographer's Daughter
Author: Anaïs Maréchal
My Review: The companion volume to A Surveyor's Notebook, written from her father's archive. Slower, sadder, and somehow even better. Maréchal is becoming the writer of her generation about what it means to inherit a vocation.

Title: The Long Pilot
Author: Helena Park
My Review: Park's argument for the year-long pilot study as the basic unit of serious software work. Persuasive and a little annoying, in the right way.

Title: Salt and Cedar
Author: Marin Espinoza
My Review: A short memoir about the year Espinoza spent rebuilding a wooden boat alone. Knows exactly what kind of book it is.

Title: Glass Trees
Author: Liesl Brandt
My Review: Pleasant. Forgettable. Brandt is better when she is funnier.

Title: The Operator's Handbook
Author: Petra Klein
My Review: Klein and Banerjee have written the book the industry needed and refused to write — a working manual for anyone who runs the systems other people get to design. Half technical reference, half ethnography, all argument that operations is the actual discipline of computing. I will be teaching from this for the next decade.

Title: Atlas of Forgotten Routes
Author: Margit Sundgren
My Review: Sundgren spent six years walking and cataloguing the abandoned trade routes of the Baltic littoral. The book is what would happen if a 19th-century gazetteer were rewritten by someone who actually loved the land. Every page is worth reading slowly.

Title: Patience
Author: Yusuf Idris
My Review: A small book about the moral cost of being in a hurry. The kind of thing you can give to someone you love without it being awkward.

Title: Refraction
Author: Devika Rao
My Review: Rao's third novel. More confident than Marrow, less startling. I will read whatever she writes next.

Title: The Rule of Three
Author: Hadiya Osei
My Review: Osei's follow-up to Slow Software. Even better. The argument is simple — that the three measures we still use for software (speed, scale, engagement) are exactly the three we should now refuse — and the book unfolds that argument across thirty-eight short chapters with a kind of quiet ferocity. Read it twice.

Title: The Slow Coast
Author: Owen Carrick
My Review: Carrick's small-format companion to Estuary. A pleasure.

Title: Bright Field
Author: Sora Tanaka
My Review: Tanaka's third novel and the best thing I have read this year so far. A novella in three movements about a marriage, a vineyard, and the year both run out at the same time. Tanaka has become the writer I most want to read next, every time.

Title: The Compiled Self
Author: Mei Lin Park
My Review: Park and Subramanian on what it means to live inside the systems we have built. The chapter on grief and version control is the strangest, best thing either of them has written.

Title: Lessons from Rome
Author: Tomas Hovden
My Review: Slight historical fiction with one excellent set piece.

Title: The Quiet Gardener
Author: Helena Park
My Review: An early Park book that already has the central argument of Quiet Compute in miniature.

Title: On Endings
Author: Idris Bah
My Review: Bah's first book and arguably his best. A 160-page argument that the last sentence of any piece of writing is a moral commitment to the reader, and that most contemporary prose breaks that commitment because the writer has not stayed long enough to know what the piece was actually about. I reread this every twelve to eighteen months.

Title: The North Light
Author: Margit Sundgren
My Review: A pleasant small book about the lighthouses of the Norwegian coast. Sundgren's second-best.

Title: Provincial
Author: Liesl Brandt
My Review: Brandt's first novel. You can see her finding her voice.

Title: Atlas of Imagined Cities
Author: Mei Lin Park
My Review: Park's first major book and the foundation of everything else she has done. A working catalogue of the cities people have built inside computers, from SimCity to the modern logistics digital twin. The chapter on the unfinished cities — projects abandoned with their populations still living inside them — is one of the strangest pieces of writing about software I know.

Title: Long Loaves
Author: Marin Espinoza
My Review: Espinoza's bread book. Everything she writes is also a book about attention.

Title: The Vanishing Drift
Author: Owen Carrick
My Review: Carrick's smallest book; still the best piece of regional history I have read this year.

Title: The Coastal Calendar
Author: Sora Tanaka
My Review: Tanaka before she was Tanaka. Already worth your time.

Title: Slow Tools
Author: Hadiya Osei
My Review: Osei's first book on the ethics of software, written before On the Ethics of Slow Software but published as a working draft. Less polished, more dangerous, equally essential.

Title: Ferry
Author: Anaïs Maréchal
My Review: A short Maréchal about the ferries of the Hebrides. Her warmest book.

Title: The Compiled City
Author: Mei Lin Park
My Review: Park's bridge book between Atlas and Field Notes. Set up the ideas she would later argue more fully.

Title: Repair
Author: Yusuf Idris
My Review: Idris on what it means to repair things — objects, relationships, civic infrastructure, an idea of the self. The argument is that the repairer is the model citizen of the next century. The book is patient enough to make the argument feel inevitable rather than romantic.

Title: The Slow Loop
Author: Petra Klein
My Review: Klein's mid-career essays on the unfashionable parts of running production systems.

Title: Marine Layer
Author: Owen Carrick
My Review: A minor Carrick. Worth it for completists.

Title: Quiet Year
Author: Marin Espinoza
My Review: Espinoza's diary of a year she spent doing nothing professional on purpose. The book is what would happen if a thoughtful person actually took a sabbatical instead of pretending to. Kind, sharp, and entirely free of advice.

Title: The Common Land
Author: Margit Sundgren
My Review: Sundgren on the surviving commons of northern Europe. A clear, generous book.

Title: Bookkeepers
Author: Liesl Brandt
My Review: Brandt finally figures out her funny side.

Title: The Telegraph Operators
Author: Petra Klein
My Review: Klein's prequel-of-sorts to Children of the Mainframe. Smaller scope; just as careful.

Title: Argentum
Author: Devika Rao
My Review: Rao's second. A more conventional novel about a more unusual subject.

Title: Foreshore
Author: Owen Carrick
My Review: Carrick on the legal and ecological history of the intertidal zone in England. Quietly brilliant.

Title: Glass Houses
Author: Helena Park
My Review: Park on the failure modes of small software. Her grumpiest book.

Title: The Long Walk Home
Author: Marisol Aguilar
My Review: Aguilar between Lighthouse and Telegraph House. Stronger than I remembered.

Title: Provisioning
Author: Idris Bah
My Review: Bah on the writing notebook. Fewer revelations than On Long Sentences but worth the afternoon.

Title: Children of the Atlas
Author: Mei Lin Park
My Review: Park's collected essays from the past decade. Useful as a single volume to lend out.

Title: Grain
Author: Marin Espinoza
My Review: Espinoza's second food book. Smaller and warmer than Long Loaves.

Title: The Working Coast
Author: Margit Sundgren
My Review: Sundgren on the small ports of the North Sea. A pleasure and a slight elegy.

Title: Lights Out
Author: Petra Klein
My Review: Klein's history of three multi-day power failures and the operators who lived through them. Beautifully reported, technically careful, and heartbreaking in the way that good operations writing always is.

Title: Marginalia
Author: Idris Bah
My Review: Bah's smallest book. A pleasure.
